statement: Pointers are a feature of C that allow for the creation of complex data structures like linked lists and trees.
Dynamic data structures can grow or shrink as needed.
Pointers enable the creation and allocation of dynamic data structures.
A structure can contain pointers, even a pointer to another instance of the same structure.
struct node { struct node *next_ptr; int value; }
The procedure malloc allocates storage for a variable and returns a pointer.
Malloc is used to create new things out of an area of memory called the heap.
To create a new node, use the malloc procedure to allocate storage for the node and return a pointer to it.
The things created by malloc can be referenced only through pointers, never by name.
The definition of malloc is: void *malloc(unsigned int);
The function malloc takes a single argument: the number of bytes to allocate.
If malloc runs out of memory, it returns a null pointer.
When used as a type in a function declaration, void indicates that the function returns no value.
When used in a pointer declaration, void defines a generic pointer.
char *string_ptr; string_ptr = malloc(80);
A struct is a collection of variables of different types that can be used to represent complex data structures.
struct person { char name[30]; char address[30]; char city_state_zip[30]; int age; float height; }
malloc is a function that allocates space on an as-needed basis.
To allocate space for a new person, use the code: new_item_ptr = malloc(sizeof(struct person));
The size of the heap is finite.
Always check the return value of each malloc call to ensure that memory was allocated.

To check the return value of malloc, use the code: if (new_item_ptr == NULL) { fprintf(stderr, "Out of memory
"); exit (8); }
The free function is used to free memory allocated by malloc.
To free memory, use the code: free(pointer); pointer = NULL;
const int DATA_SIZE = (16 * 1024); void copy(void) { char *data_ptr; data_ptr = malloc(DATA_SIZE); /* Use the data buffer to copy a file */ free(data_ptr); data_ptr = NULL; }
Always free allocated memory when it is no longer needed to prevent memory leaks.
Using memory that has been freed can cause unexpected results or program crashes.
A linked list is a chain of items in which each item points to the next one in the chain.
Linked lists can grow dynamically as more items are added.
The structure declarations for a linked list are: struct linked_list { char data[30]; struct linked_list *next_ptr; };
The variable first_ptr points to the first element of the list and is initialized to NULL when the list is empty.
To insert a new element into a linked list in C, create a structure for the item using new_item_ptr = malloc(sizeof(struct linked_list));
Store the item in the new element using (*new_item_ptr).data = item;
Make the first element of the list point to the new element using (*new_item_ptr).next_ptr = first_ptr;
Allocate memory for a new linked list item using malloc(sizeof(struct linked_list))
Copy the item to be added into the new linked list item using strcpy((*new_item_ptr).data, item)
To search for a name in a linked list, start at the first element and compare each element's data to the target name until a match is found or the end of the list is reached.
The find function in Example 17-2 searches through the items in a linked list to find a specific name, returning 1 if the name is found and 0 if it is not.
The find function uses a while loop to traverse the linked list, comparing each element's data to the target name using the strcmp function.
When searching a linked list, it is necessary to check for the end of the list (i.e., a null pointer) to avoid dereferencing a null pointer.
The structure pointer (->) operator is a shorthand for accessing the field of a structure pointer.
The dot (.) operator indicates the field of a structure, while the -> operator indicates the field of a structure pointer.
The expressions (*current_ptr).data = value; and current_ptr->data = value; are equivalent.
To insert an element into an ordered linked list, first locate the insert point by moving a variable along the list until the proper place for the insert is found.
void enter(struct item *first_ptr, const int value) { ... }
while (1) { if (after_ptr == NULL) break; if (after_ptr->value >= value) break; after_ptr = after_ptr->next_ptr; before_ptr = before_ptr->next_ptr; }
new_item_ptr = malloc(sizeof(struct item)); new_item_ptr->value = value; before_ptr->next_ptr = new_item_ptr; new_item_ptr->next_ptr = after_ptr;
The new element must be linked in between before_ptr and after_ptr
before_ptr->next_ptr = new_item_ptr
A double-linked list contains two links, one pointing forward to the next element and the other pointing backward to the previous element
A double-linked list is a data structure with two links: one forward and one backward.
The structure for a double-linked list is: struct double_list { int data; struct double_list *next_ptr; struct double_list *previous_ptr; };

The code to insert a new element in a double-linked list involves iterating through the list to find the correct position, as shown in the double_enter function: void double_enter(struct double_list *head_ptr, int item) { ... }
The double_enter function does not handle the case where the element is inserted at the head or end of the list.
if (item >= insert_ptr->data) break;
In a double-linked list, the previous pointer of a new node is set to the previous pointer of the insertion point node.
Unlike single-linked lists, double-linked lists do not require a before_ptr to point to the element in front of the insert point.

To adjust the links of the old elements in a double-linked list insert, start at insert_ptr and follow the link previous_ptr to the previous element, then update its next_ptr field to point to the new element using the code: insert_ptr->previous_ptr->next_ptr = new_ptr;
A binary tree is a data type that can be used to create an alphabetized list of words, allowing for efficient searching and insertion.
Searching a linked list is slow because each element must be checked until the correct insertion point is found.
Using a tree data type can cut the number of compares down tremendously compared to a linked list.
A tree is a data structure where each box (node) contains two pointers, a left pointer and a right pointer, that point to the left and right subtrees.
The structure for a tree is: struct node { char *data; struct node *left; struct node *right; };
Trees are often used for storing a symbol table, a list of variables used in a program.
The advantage of a tree over a linked list is that searching a tree takes considerably less time.
To search for a word in a tree, start at the root and descend to the left or right subtree based on whether the word is less than or greater than the current word.
For example, to find the word 'orange' in a tree, start at the root 'lemon', descend to the right link to 'pear', and then descend to the left link to 'orange'.
The function must make things simpler for recursion to be useful.
There must be some endpoint for recursion to terminate.
If the current tree (or subtree) is null, create a one-node tree with the given word.
If the current node contains the word, do nothing during tree insertion.
Perform a recursive call to insert the word in the left or right subtree based on the word's value.
Inserting the word 'fig' into the tree involves comparing it with 'lemon', then 'apple', and finally 'grape', resulting in the creation of a new node.
void enter(struct node **node, char *word) { ... }
if ((*node) == NULL) { (*node) = malloc(sizeof(struct node)); }
if ((*node) == NULL)  memory_error();
(*node)->left = NULL; (*node)->right = NULL; (*node)->word = save_string(word);
result = strcmp((*node)->word, word);
When changing the value of a pointer, we must pass a pointer to the pointer.
if (result < 0)  enter(&(*node)->right, word); else  enter(&(*node)->left, word);
The printing algorithm for a tree uses recursion.
Print the data that comes before this node (left tree), then print this node and print the data that comes after this node (right tree)
void print_tree(struct node *top) { if (top == NULL) return; print_tree(top->left); printf("%s\n", top->word); print_tree(top->right); }
The isalpha macro returns nonzero if its argument is a letter and otherwise
The program handles the out-of-memory problem by writing an error message and quitting
The save_string function creates space for a string on the heap and returns the pointer to it
A struct node represents a tree node with a left child, a right child, and a word.
The struct node is defined as: struct node { struct node *left; struct node *right; char *word; };
To save a string on the heap, allocate memory using malloc and copy the string into it: new_string = malloc((unsigned) (strlen(string) + 1)); strcpy(new_string, string);

The memory_error function writes an error message and exits the program: void memory_error(void) { fprintf(stderr, "Error:Out of memory
"); exit(8); }
If the current node is null, allocate memory for a new node using malloc(sizeof(struct node)) and initialize its left and right child pointers to NULL.
If the comparison result is 0, the word is already in the tree, so no entry is necessary.
If the comparison result is less than 0, recursively call the enter function on the right subtree.
The enter function uses a recursive approach to traverse the tree and insert a new word.
void scan(char *name) { ... }
in_file = fopen(name, "r");
ch = fgetc(in_file);
Always check if the file was opened successfully before proceeding.
The fscanf function returns EOF when the end of the file is reached.
The fopen function returns a NULL pointer if the file cannot be opened.
Trees are supposed to be fast for searching and other operations.
A tree should be constructed in a way that minimizes search time, such as through balancing.
To print a tree, recursively traverse the left subtree, print the current node's value, and then traverse the right subtree.

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) { if (argc != 2) { fprintf(stderr, "Error:Wrong number of parameters
"); ... } }
A tree data structure is used to represent multiple possible moves and responses in a chess-playing program.
The number of branches for each node in the chess tree can be more than two.
struct chess { struct board board; struct next { struct move; struct *chess_ptr; } next[MAX_MOVES]; };
The number of possible moves from any given position in a chess game can vary dramatically.
In the beginning of a chess game, there are many possible moves, while in the end game, the number of possible moves is greatly reduced.
A linked list is a data structure that allocates storage on an on-demand basis.
Using a linked list results in a more efficient use of storage.
In a chess program, a linked list can be used to store possible moves, where the list grows only as needed, resulting in a more efficient use of storage.
To implement a linked list, define a struct with a pointer to the next element, and allocate storage for each element on an as-needed basis.
Check if a pointer is valid (not NULL) before checking what it is pointing to.
while (current_ptr != NULL) { if (strcmp(current_ptr->data, name) == 0) break; }
An ordered linked list can degenerate into a linked list, where only one branch is used.
Balancing a binary tree can prevent it from degenerating into a linked list.
